result:
At dawn, the city looks as if it has been returned to the world by mistake. Delivery trucks hum along the avenues with the modest authority of beetles. A baker lifts a metal gate, and warm air spills out carrying the first rumor of bread. Pigeons conduct their usual meeting beside a puddle, each one pretending to have discovered an urgent matter of state. Above them, windows brighten one by one, not with drama, but with the patient click of kettles, lamps, showers, radios.

I like this hour because ambition has not yet put on its shoes. The day is still a loose sketch. Emails are unsent. Apologies might still be made. A notebook can open to a blank page without accusing anyone. Even the trees along the sidewalk seem less like scenery than witnesses, holding their branches over everything with a grave and generous calm.

Soon the engines will grow louder, and people will become their destinations. They will fold themselves into trains, argue with traffic lights, buy coffee, misplace keys, remember appointments, forget dreams. But for a few minutes the world offers a simpler contract: breathe in, notice, begin. That is enough. The light climbs the brick walls, a dog shakes rain from its ears, and somewhere a stranger laughs before the day has told him why. I keep walking, carrying no special wisdom, only a cup warming my hands and the small, stubborn belief that attention is a kind of thanks for being here at all today.

A baker lifts a metal gate, and warm air spills out carrying the first rumor of bread. Pigeons conduct their usual meeting beside a puddle, each one pretending to have discovered an urgent matter of state. Above them, windows brighten one by one, not with drama, but with the patient click of kettles, lamps, showers, radios.

I like this hour because ambition has not yet put on its shoes. The day is still a loose sketch. Emails are unsent. Apologies might still be made. A notebook can open to a blank page without accusing anyone. Even the trees along the sidewalk seem less like scenery than witnesses, holding their branches over everything with a grave and generous calm.

Soon the engines will grow louder, and people will become their destinations. They will fold themselves into trains, argue with traffic lights, buy coffee, misplace keys, remember appointments, forget dreams. But for a few minutes the world offers a simpler contract: breathe in, notice, begin. That is enough. The light climbs the brick walls, a dog shakes rain from its ears, and somewhere a stranger laughs before the day has told him why. I keep walking, carrying no special wisdom, only a cup warming my hands and the small, stubborn belief that attention is a kind of thanks for being here at all today.
